title: Shakshuka
subtitle: Eggs poached in spiced tomato and pepper sauce
excerpt: Whole eggs cracked into a wide pan of simmering tomato sauce, spiced with cumin, paprika, and chilli, then covered and cooked until the whites are set but the yolks remain runny. Served straight from the pan with bread for scooping. One of the few dishes that is as good for dinner as it is for breakfast.
sourceNotes: Shakshuka is a Levantine and North African dish with origins in Tunisia and wide adoption across the Arab world, Israel, and the broader Middle East. Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) includes variations, and it became widely known internationally through Yotam Ottolenghi and Sami Tamimi's 'Jerusalem' (2012). The method of poaching eggs directly in tomato sauce is also documented in Algerian and Libyan cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tomato sauce can be made up to 2 days ahead. Reheat until simmering before adding the eggs.

content.text: The pan matters more than the recipe. Use a wide, lidded frying pan so the sauce is shallow enough to concentrate quickly and the eggs sit in it rather than floating. A 30 cm pan is about right for four eggs. The sauce should be reduced and thick before the eggs go in — if it is too loose, the whites will spread and the yolks will set before the whites are cooked through.

The eggs need a lid and 6 to 8 minutes of gentle heat. Check at 6 minutes: the white should be just set, the yolk still visibly liquid when you shake the pan. If you like a fully set yolk, leave it for another 2 minutes but do not walk away — the transition from set-white-runny-yolk to fully set happens fast. Serve with bread that can cope with the sauce: pitta, crusty bread, or a soft roll.

items.prepNote: finely sliced, or 0.5 tsp chilli flakes

content.text: Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and {{pepper-red}} and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until softened and beginning to colour. Add the {{garlic}}, {{chilli-red}} if using, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{paprika-sweet}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tomato-puree}} and stir for another minute. Pour in the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, crush them with a spoon, season with salt, and simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce has thickened and reduced slightly.

Make four shallow wells in the sauce with the back of a spoon. Crack one {{eggs}} into each well. Season the eggs lightly with salt. Cover the pan with a lid and cook over low heat for 6 to 8 minutes until the whites are just set and the yolks remain runny. Scatter parsley over the top if using. Serve directly from the pan with bread alongside.

items.symptom: Whites set before yolks are cooked
items.cause: Sauce too thin and the egg whites are submerged rather than sitting on top
items.fix: Reduce the sauce for longer before adding the eggs — it should be thick enough that the egg sits on the surface. If the sauce is too loose, the bottom of the white cooks faster than the top

items.cause: Lid on too long or heat too high
items.fix: Check at 6 minutes and remove the lid as soon as the whites are opaque — the yolks can go from runny to solid in 2 minutes over high heat
Simmer the sauce uncovered for 10 to 15 minutes before adding the eggs — it should be thick and jammy, not loose and soupy

items.cause: Insufficient sauce depth or the eggs landed on the pan surface
items.fix: Make sure the wells in the sauce are deep enough that the egg sits in sauce, not on bare pan. Add a splash of water if the sauce is too dry

content.text: Shakshuka's origins are disputed with the energy that the Middle East brings to food questions: Tunisia claims it, Libya claims it, and Israel adopted it so thoroughly that it became a national breakfast dish in the twentieth century. The name likely derives from an Arabic root meaning 'all mixed up', which describes the technique. It is the kind of dish that exists in every country in the region under different names and with different spice profiles — the Tunisian version is more assertively spiced, the Israeli version is milder, the Yemeni version uses zhug and is much hotter. What they share is the method: eggs in tomato sauce, lid on, patience.
